stack: Pure static site, zero build step, no framework/bundler/npm. Each page is one self-contained HTML file with inline <style> and one inline vanilla-JS IIFE. External deps only via CDN: Google Fonts and TensorFlow.js 4.17.0 (`<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@tensorflow/tfjs@4.17.0/dist/tf.min.js">`). The real MobileNetV2 classifier (Keras .h5, 97% val acc) is converted with tensorflowjs_converter to a TF.js LayersModel and runs fully in-browser (WebGL). Python side (TF 2.15 in a dedicated .venv-tfjs) only exports assets offline. Local preview: `cd docs && python3 -m http.server 8000`.
fileStructure:
Repo: /Users/NavSha/Claude_Projects/dogs-vs-cats (branch: master). Site root is docs/ (12MB total):
- docs/index.html (~585 lines, 44K) — the deployed 5-chapter dark scroll-driven exhibit; fully self-contained (all CSS+JS inline)
- docs/editorial.html (1030 lines) — light "editorial" variant of the same explainer, uses styles/shared.css widget kit
- docs/lab.html (1527 lines) — "Raise the Machine", a standalone in-browser NN laboratory (trains models live with tf.sequential/.fit)
- docs/styles/shared.css (1409 lines) — design system, ported from the interactive-explainers site
- docs/model/ — TF.js LayersModel of the full classifier: model.json + 3 weight shards (2x 4MB + 1.9MB, ~10MB total)
- docs/model-activations/ — small multi-output activation sub-model (model.json + 1 shard, ~240K) returning 3 early-layer feature maps
- docs/data/ — training_history.json (real per-epoch TensorBoard history, phase1/phase2) and predictions.json (Python-side ground-truth scores for verifying the browser model)
- docs/samples/ — cat.jpg, dog.jpg default specimens; docs/lab-data/ — ~240 jpgs + manifest.json for lab.html; docs/screenshots/hero.png; docs/.nojekyll
- src/export/export_web_assets.py — the export pipeline (builds activation sub-model .h5, dumps predictions + TB history JSON); conversion commands documented in README.md
designSystem: index.html (dark exhibit): CSS custom properties in :root — bg #080b12, surfaces #121a2b/#0c1320 with linear-gradient card fills, text #E7ECF5, dim #8A93A6, line #1e2840; accents cyan #22D3EE (primary), indigo #818CF8, amber #F59E0B (warnings), red #F87171. Fixed radial-gradient glows on body background. Fonts: Space Grotesk (display headings, clamp() fluid sizes e.g. clamp(42px,7vw,92px), letter-spacing -.03em), Inter (body 17px / line-height 1.7), JetBrains Mono (kickers/labels, uppercase, letter-spacing .2-.28em). Signature elements: gradient-clipped <em> text in H1; 3px fixed top progress rail (cyan→indigo gradient); centered .chapter-head bands with mono chapter numbers; .note__box callouts with 3px left border in three variants (cyan=idea, amber=warn, indigo=summary); pill .chip buttons with active state; .badge outro pill; shared easing cubic-bezier(.16,1,.3,1); prefers-reduced-motion kill-switch. editorial.html + shared.css (light variant): paper palette #FAF8F5/#FFFFFF, Newsreader serif, 8px spacing scale (--space-1..24), 760px content/widget width, and a multi-topic accent-token system (accent-vision/cost/eval/context/llm/grounding/ux/vibe, each with base/deep/light/gradient) where a new theme is applied by remapping its colors onto the base family (--accent-agents) so all base components retheme automatically. lab.html has a third theme (phosphor green #53F1B6 on near-black, Fraunces serif, film-grain overlay, HUD chrome).
scrollytellingApproach: Hand-rolled, no library (no Scrollama/GSAP) — about 30 lines of JS plus CSS. Layout: .scene is a 2-column grid; .scene__visual is position:sticky top:0 height:100vh (pinned canvas "stage"); .scene__steps holds tall .step blocks (min-height 86vh) that scroll past it; .scene--right flips column order. Trigger: ONE IntersectionObserver over all .step elements with rootMargin '-45% 0px -45% 0px' (a center screen band); intersecting steps get .active (opacity/translate transition on .step__card) and dispatch (data-ch, data-step) to per-chapter callbacks registered via registerSteps(ch, cb) — each chapter renders its pinned visual as a pure function of the current step index. A separate one-shot helper onEnter(sel, cb, margin) lazily kicks off heavy work (fetch training JSON, run occlusion experiment) when a section approaches the viewport. Scroll progress rail updated on passive scroll listener. Mobile <860px: grid collapses to one column, the visual becomes a sticky 52vh header above the prose, steps drop min-height and are forced always-visible (opacity:1) since the center-band pattern is janky on phones.
widgetArchitecture: Core pattern in docs/index.html: a single shared "specimen" — an offscreen 150x150 canvas that IS the app state. A tiny pub/sub (onSpecimen(fn)/emitSpecimen()) makes all five chapters recompute whenever the photo changes (sample chips or file upload; uploads drawn center-cropped to the canvas, never leave the browser). Models are lazy memoized promises: classifier() and activations() call tf.loadLayersModel('model/model.json' / 'model-activations/model.json') on first use only, so the 10MB download never blocks first paint. runExclusive(fn) is a promise-chain mutex serializing all tfjs work so chapters triggered by scroll never run inference concurrently. Per chapter: Ch1 (pixels) — pure canvas, hover reads RGB via getImageData on the specimen (willReadFrequently:true). Ch2 (patterns) — the trick: a multi-output activation sub-model exported offline from Python (layers Conv1_relu / block_2_expand_relu / block_5_expand_relu = edges/textures/patterns, capped at block_5 to keep weights tiny) returns 3 feature maps in one predict; channel-mean composites are min-max normalized, painted through a cyan color ramp onto a mix-blend-mode:screen glow canvas, plus top-4 channel thumbnail chips. Ch3 (attention) — occlusion sensitivity computed live: 8x8 grid of grey patches over the image, 65 predictions batched in chunks of 8 with setTimeout(0) yields and a progress counter, heatmap = confidence drop per cell, plus a generated plain-English sentence ("hiding the upper left confused it most"). Ch4 (training) — NO model: precomputed docs/data/training_history.json (parsed from real TensorBoard event files by src/export/export_web_assets.py), drawn as a dependency-free canvas line chart whose reveal is driven by scroll step (line grows to epoch N, dashed fine-tune boundary appears, then peak annotation). Ch5 (break it) — generates adversarial inputs on canvas (random noise, solid color) or accepts any upload, classifies live, templated explanation keyed to input kind and confidence. So model data is hybrid: live tfjs for anything touching the user's image, precomputed JSON for the training narrative and verification (predictions.json).
failureChapterPattern: Chapter 5 "Break it" + outro. Structure: (1) framing copy that sets up the limitation ("it has only ever learned two words"); (2) one-click OOD generators — 'TV static' (random-pixel canvas), 'Blank square' (solid fill), and an open upload labeled "a car, a fox, your face" — so users can sabotage the model with zero effort; (3) the REAL model runs and the UI leans into the failure: big verdict line with confidence percentage and an animated confidence bar; (4) a switch on (input kind, confidence) picks a contextual plain-English lesson — noise: "pure random static... it still picked a side, and sounded sure"; solid: "forced it into the nearest box it owns"; confident upload (>85%): "high confidence means 'this resembles my training,' not 'I am right'"; hedged: "it can never answer 'neither'". (5) The outro section then generalizes the felt experience into three product lessons in a summary callout: confidence is not correctness; a model only knows its training world; "I don't know" has to be designed in. The pattern is: interactive sabotage -> observed confident failure -> generalized takeaway, and it works because the user caused the failure themselves.
deploySetup: GitHub Pages, legacy "deploy from branch" mode (confirmed via gh api repos/NavSha/dogs-vs-cats/pages): branch master, path /docs, no GitHub Actions workflow at all. docs/.nojekyll disables Jekyll processing. Publishing = commit to master and push; git history shows an occasional empty "Retrigger GitHub Pages deployment" commit was needed to force a rebuild. Asset pipeline is manual and offline: `python src/export/export_web_assets.py` (in .venv-tfjs: tensorflow==2.15.1 + tensorflowjs==4.17.0) then two `tensorflowjs_converter --input_format=keras` runs into docs/model and docs/model-activations. Live at https://navsha.github.io/dogs-vs-cats/.
gotchas: The tensorflowjs pip package pulls a TF/Keras stack that clashes with the training venv — README mandates a separate .venv-tfjs pinned to tensorflow==2.15.1 + tensorflowjs==4.17.0 matching the training versions.

A `data/` .gitignore rule silently excluded docs/data/*.json from commits, breaking Chapter 4 in production; fixed by anchoring the rule to repo root (commit 3d63082). Check gitignore anchoring when a site dir shadows a data dir name.
TF.js runtime version in the <script> tag (4.17.0) is deliberately matched to the converter version that exported the model.
Pages deploys from branch `master` (not main) at /docs; legacy Pages occasionally needs an empty commit to retrigger a build.

Concurrent tfjs inference from multiple scroll-triggered widgets thrashes WebGL — the runExclusive() promise-chain mutex serializes all model work; every tensor path uses tf.tidy/dispose to avoid GPU memory leaks.
Canvases that are read back need getContext('2d',{willReadFrequently:true}); loaded sample images need crossOrigin='anonymous' to avoid tainting the canvas for getImageData.
The 65-inference occlusion loop must be chunked (batches of 8) with await setTimeout(0) yields and a visible progress counter, or it freezes the main thread.

Mobile (<860px): the center-band IntersectionObserver + 86vh steps pattern degrades, so steps are forced always-visible and the sticky visual becomes a 52vh header — design the mobile fallback explicitly.
Model download is ~10MB — acceptable only because loading is lazy (defer script + memoized loadLayersModel promise on first interaction) so the hero renders instantly.
prefers-reduced-motion is globally respected (scroll-behavior:auto + near-zero transition durations).

Narrative honesty needed dedicated commits ('Ch4 why is the studied line lower' amber callout for augmentation/dropout; 'make the layer-depth claims honest' for Ch2) — plan callouts that preempt reader confusion about real data quirks.
reusableForMlPapers: The whole zero-build architecture: one self-contained HTML file per exhibit (inline CSS + one IIFE), docs/ as Pages root with .nojekyll, deploy-from-branch — ideal for ml-papers where each paper could be its own standalone page sharing a design system.

The scrollytelling kernel, copy nearly verbatim from docs/index.html: .scene sticky-grid CSS (lines ~68-86 plus the <860px mobile block at ~158-169) and the JS at lines ~422-427 (single IntersectionObserver with rootMargin '-45% 0px -45% 0px', registerSteps(ch,cb) dispatch, onEnter() one-shot lazy trigger). ~30 lines total, no library.

The dark design-token set: :root palette (#080b12 bg, #22D3EE/#818CF8 cyan-indigo accents), Space Grotesk / Inter / JetBrains Mono trio, mono uppercase kickers, gradient-clipped em in H1, fixed radial-glow body background, gradient progress rail, .chapter-head bands, .note__box callout variants (idea/warn/summary), .chip pill buttons, .badge outro pill.

The 'specimen' pattern: one shared state object + onSpecimen/emitSpecimen pub/sub so every widget on the page reacts to one user choice — for ml-papers this maps to 'one chosen example/prompt/paper flows through all visualizations'.
runExclusive() promise mutex + memoized lazy model loaders (classifier()/activations()) — mandatory plumbing if any ml-papers page runs tfjs/onnx demos triggered by scroll.

The precomputed-JSON widget pattern (Ch4): heavy computation done offline in Python (src/export/export_web_assets.py parses real TensorBoard logs) -> tiny JSON in docs/data/ -> dependency-free canvas chart progressively revealed by scroll step. For ml-papers, precompute per-paper artifacts (loss curves, attention maps, benchmark tables) as JSON instead of shipping models.

The Ch4 canvas line-chart function (draw() in index.html ~lines 537-557): gridlines, axis labels, phase-boundary dashed line, peak annotation, progressive reveal — a reusable no-dependency chart for training-curve stories (AlexNet, scaling laws, etc.).

The 'Break it' failure-chapter template + generalizing outro — a strong recurring closer for each paper page (e.g. 'where attention fails', 'what scaling laws don't predict'): one-click sabotage inputs, real observed failure, then a summary callout of transferable lessons.

The shared.css multi-accent token system (per-topic accent families remapped onto one base family so components retheme automatically) — ml-papers could give each era/paper-category its own accent while sharing all base widget components; also its .widget/.widget__label/.widget__title/.widget__instruction kit as the widget chrome convention.

lab.html patterns if ml-papers wants live training demos: tf.sequential head trained in-browser with .fit callbacks, dataset as a folder of jpgs + manifest.json, plus its distinctive HUD/experiment-log page chrome.
README convention: hero screenshot linking to the live site, numbered chapter list with one-line hooks, and exact reproducible asset-rebuild commands.
